Title: Drug syndicates using last-minute airport check-ins to evade detection, say police
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: International drug syndicates are exploiting last-minute and peak-hour airport check-ins to slip drug mules past authorities.
Bukit Aman Narcotics Crime Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said syndicates deliberately exploit busy periods in the hope of evading airport security and screening systems.
"Among their tactics is checking in at the last minute or during peak hours," he said when contacted today.
Hussein said monitoring shows airports remain the primary route for drug mules, particularly those involved with international syndicates.
He added that other entry points such as ports and land borders are also used, through different modus operandi compared with airport drug mules.
"Authorities, particularly the NCID, are constantly assessing current trends and strengthening controls across all national entry points," he said.
He described the use of Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) in Sepang as a transit hub for international drug syndicates — especially those using foreign tourists as mules — as a serious threat requiring continuous monitoring.
Malaysia's strategic location as a regional aviation hub makes KLIA a preferred transit route for cross-continental drug smuggling, he noted.
"However, the NCID, together with other enforcement agencies in the country, is continuously strengthening security controls, intelligence, and screening to ensure every smuggling attempt is detected and foiled," he said.
Hussein added that syndicates are employing increasingly sophisticated and structured tactics, including offering fake job opportunities, free holiday packages, and lucrative financial rewards.
Victims are often tricked into carrying items without knowing the actual contents.
"In addition, there are cases where drugs are creatively hidden inside luggage, clothing, personal items, and even through body packing to evade detection," he said.
He advised the public to remain vigilant and avoid suspicious offers, warning never to carry items belonging to others without knowing their contents, especially at airports.
"Involvement in such activities can lead to severe penalties, including life imprisonment or the death penalty in certain countries," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kuala Pilah traders seek jobs, growth over election promises
Article:
KUALA PILAH: As political parties criss-cross Negri Sembilan in search of votes ahead of the state election, traders in Kuala Pilah are hoping less for political rhetoric and more for meaningful change that will revive the district's economy and ease the rising cost of living.
Long described by residents as among the slowest districts to develop in the state, many are hoping the next government will do more than build infrastructure.
They want sustained economic growth that creates quality jobs for young people, giving them reasons to remain in Negri Sembilan instead of seeking opportunities elsewhere.
For flower garland maker Mira, elections may bring a temporary increase in customers, but the real challenge is making enough to sustain her business once the campaign banners come down.
She has been operating her business for 16 years, including the past 12 years at her current premises near the Kuala Pilah bus terminal.
She specialises in making flower garlands, saying the niche trade has enabled her to stay afloat while many neighbouring businesses have folded.
"If I only sold flowers, I would have closed long ago. Making garlands is what keeps the business going because there are not many people doing it," she said.
Mira said Kuala Pilah's commercial centre no longer enjoyed the lively atmosphere it once did, with many long-established shops remaining shuttered as traders struggled with rising operating costs and competition from online sellers.
"It is not like before. Business has become slow.
"People can buy things much cheaper online now. Clothes that we sell for RM50 can be bought for far less through online platforms. It is difficult to compete."
She gestured towards rows of closed shoplots surrounding her premises as evidence of the town's slowing economy.
"Many shops have closed. People say they no longer have the capital to continue because everything has become more expensive."
Mira also believes Kuala Pilah lost much of its foot traffic after long-distance bus services stopped operating from the old bus terminal.
"Previously, buses from many places stopped here. Now there are only local bus services. Without the crowds, there are fewer customers."
While election periods bring campaign workers and visitors to town, providing a modest boost for businesses, she said it was not enough to offset rising costs.
"The biggest burden is fuel prices. When fuel goes up, transport costs rise, and flower prices increase too because we source them from Cameron Highlands.
"When suppliers increase prices, we also have to raise ours. Even if we increase it by just 20 or 30 sen, customers complain. But we have no choice if we want to continue making a profit."
Despite the challenges, Mira said she had only one wish for the next state government.
"I don't mind who wins. The most important thing is that the country remains peaceful. We accept whatever the people decide, as long as there is peace."
Nearby, rental car driver Ramli Hassan, 62, who took up the job after retiring from government service, said Kuala Pilah had long lagged behind other districts in terms of development.
"Development has always been slow here. Compared with other districts in Negri Sembilan, Kuala Pilah is among the slowest. There is development, but it takes time," he said.
Ramli, who previously spent more than three decades as a government school bus driver before retiring, said earning a living as a rental car driver had become increasingly difficult.
"It depends on your luck. Sometimes you can wait two or three hours without getting a passenger. If I earn RM10 after waiting three hours, I consider myself fortunate.
"People spend more than RM10 on a meal these days. Most people own cars now, so fewer use rental cars."
He believes Kuala Pilah has untapped potential but requires stronger promotion and sustained investment.
Asked what visitors should experience in the district, Ramli recommended its long-established cendol and rojak stalls in the town centre, as well as attractions such as Ulu Bendul Recreational Forest and Batu Baling.
Among those hoping for a more business-friendly environment is apam balik seller Taufiq Yusrizal, 22, who said the next state government should focus on creating more job opportunities while reducing the burden on small traders.
He said authorities should simplify procedures for obtaining business licences and permits, describing the current process as cumbersome for small entrepreneurs trying to earn a living.
"Make it easier for small traders. Whether it is applying for licences or paying for permits, the process should be more straightforward," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Berjasa's Zamani vows to improve housing, education, and healthcare in Nilai
Article:
SEREMBAN: Berjasa candidate for the Nilai seat Zamani Ibrahim has pledged to champion affordable housing, local jobs, and better education and healthcare if elected.
The party's sole candidate in the Negri Sembilan election, the 62-year-old said development in the fast-growing constituency must bring greater benefits to its residents.
Zamani, who is Berjasa president, said being a first-time electoral candidate should not be seen as a disadvantage.
"I believe the people of Nilai should vote for me even though I am new. Being new does not necessarily mean lacking experience. In fact, newcomers often bring fresh ideas," he said in an interview after appearing as a guest on Bicara PRN Negri Sembilan.
He said one of his priorities was ensuring development in Nilai directly benefited residents, particularly through affordable housing.
While acknowledging the constituency's rapid growth, Zamani questioned whether new housing projects were meeting the needs of the local community.
"Another concern frequently raised during my visits to Felda settlements is the future of second- and third-generation Felda settlers.
"Although Nilai offers numerous employment opportunities, many jobs are filled by workers from outside the constituency, while rising property prices have made home ownership increasingly difficult for locals.
"Many homes are now priced at around RM650,000, making them unaffordable for residents."
He said he would prioritise housing projects for Nilai residents by providing more affordable homes if elected.
Zamani also identified employment as a key issue, noting that despite the presence of many thriving factories, local youths continued to seek jobs outside the constituency.
He described the situation as a missed opportunity and said policies should better connect residents with employment opportunities available in Nilai.
Education is another priority, he said, pointing to overcrowded classrooms in government schools despite the township's rapid development.
Drawing on his background in education, Zamani said he intended to leverage his professional network, including former students now serving in Putrajaya, to strengthen educational opportunities in the constituency.
He also pledged to improve healthcare services, citing his ties with medical professionals and non-governmental organisations, which he said could help support efforts to upgrade the Nilai Health Clinic into a more comprehensive healthcare facility.
Zamri is set to face incumbent J. Arul Kumar (Pakatan Harapan), Datuk Lai Chien Kong (Barisan Nasional), Datuk V. Saravana Kumar (Bersatu) and independent candidate Omar Mohd Isa in a five-cornered contest.
On claims that his candidacy could split the vote, Zamani dismissed the suggestion, saying he was not campaigning in isolation despite contesting under the Berjasa banner.
"I do not believe it is accurate to say that I am splitting the vote.
"From the perspective of the party logo, I may appear to be contesting alone. But in terms of campaign operations, I am not alone."
He said he had met various political leaders to build support for Berjasa and that his campaign was backed by a strong network of supporters.
"The media may see me as contesting independently because of the logo, but in reality my campaign is supported by many people."
Zamani said he had also sent an official letter to Perikatan Nasional chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar before entering the race to inform him of his intention to contest.
He acknowledged that his campaign had trailed those of more established parties during the first five days of the election period, attributing the slower rollout of flags and posters to printing delays caused by high demand.
"Compared with parties that have been established for many years, I admit we are behind.
"However, the party's campaign machinery has already been organised, with more than 200 volunteers mobilised, and I am confident our campaign operations will be in full swing by the end of the week."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rising costs, medical brain drain top agenda at APHM conference
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Association of Private Hospitals of Malaysia (APHM) will host its 32nd International Healthcare Conference and Exhibition from July 28 to 30, 2026, at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre (KLCC).
Themed "From Volume to Value: Measuring What Matters in Healthcare Performance", the event will be launched by Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad.
In a statement, APHM said the conference comes at a critical juncture as global healthcare systems, including Malaysia's, grapple with escalating costs, a "brain drain" of medical professionals and mounting pressure to maintain affordability.
"The traditional volume-driven model of care, which focuses on the quantity of patients treated, is reaching its limit, necessitating an urgent shift towards delivering better clinical value," it said.
This focus aligns with APHM's ongoing priority to address rising medical costs.
The association has been working closely with the government through the Joint Ministerial Committee on Private Healthcare Costs to address these systemic challenges.
The three-day programme is structured to address the core facets of healthcare reform.
The opening discourse will examine the relationship between regulators and payers to foster sustainable financing and ecosystem-wide trust.
Further discussions will focus on the "human dimension" of healthcare, emphasising the interplay between clinical outcomes, patient experience and workforce engagement.
The convention will conclude by highlighting operational excellence, specifically how digital transformation and smarter resource management can drive hospital performance without compromising quality of care.
APHM president Datuk Dr Kuljit Singh said while Malaysia had earned a reputation for high-quality healthcare and remained a leading medical tourism destination, the industry must continue to evolve.
"As healthcare challenges continue to change, we must move beyond measuring success solely by volume and focus instead on delivering greater value," he said.
He said the conference would serve as a platform for healthcare leaders from Malaysia and abroad to share best practices and develop practical solutions to translate value into everyday patient care.
Running concurrently with the conference is 'tHIS ASEAN' (The Health Industry Series) exhibition.
Co-organised with Reed Sinopharm Exhibitions, the showcase will feature the latest innovations in medical devices, pharmaceuticals, hospital construction and healthcare technology.
It aims to provide industry players with a business-to-business (B2B) platform to network and establish strategic partnerships.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pemadam: Rehab, not arrest numbers, the true measure in war on drugs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Requiring every police station chief to arrest at least one drug addict each day is a proactive move that deserves praise, but it should not be the sole benchmark in the fight against drugs.
Malaysian Association for the Prevention of Drugs (Pemadam) secretary-general Hasnul Fairuz Hushin Amri said the true measure of success was not the number of arrests made, but whether drug abuse was declining and syndicates were being dismantled.
He said the fight against drugs should be judged on how effectively the entire supply chain is disrupted – from top-level syndicates to end users.
"I view the Kelantan police chief's directive requiring every police station chief to make at least
one drug-related arrest each day
as
a proactive measure to strengthen enforcement and improve accountability at the grassroots level.
"However, success in combating drugs cannot be measured solely by the number of arrests because the real achievement lies in disrupting the drug supply chain," he said.
<>WAR ON DRUGS REQUIRES UNITED FRONT
Hasnul said efforts to curb drug abuse require close cooperation among all relevant agencies and the public, as the responsibility cannot rest solely with the authorities.
"Combating drug abuse requires the development of a strong and sustainable ecosystem.
"No single agency can address this issue on its own.
"It requires strategic cooperation between the police, the National Anti-Drugs Agency, the Education Ministry, the Health Ministry, non-governmental organisations (NGOs), local authorities, religious institutions, community leaders and the media.
"Each party has its own role in prevention, education, enforcement, treatment, rehabilitation and the reintegration of former drug
addicts into society.
"Pemadam believes the best approach is a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach, where all government agencies and every segment of society work together as one team," he said.
Hasnul said once a comprehensive and well-established ecosystem is in place, prevention, intervention and rehabilitation efforts can be accelerated.
He said this would also enable more comprehensive support to be provided not only to drug addicts seeking recovery, but also to their
families.
"Only through effective prevention, consistent enforcement and strong collaborative networks can we realise the goal of building a safer, healthier Malaysia free from the threat of drug abuse.
"Our goal is not merely to increase arrest statistics, but to build a society that is resilient against the threat of drugs," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Network School shifts to Kazakhstan after Johor authorities halt operations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Network School, which previously operated in Forest City, Johor, is set to establish a new campus in Kazakhstan.
In a post on X, founder Balaji Srinivasan announced that a memorandum of understanding (MoU) had been signed between Network School and the Republic of Kazakhstan to develop the campus.
He said the campus was envisioned as a hub for global technology talent, offering expedited visa processing, streamlined relocation support and active talent recruitment.
"I am pleased to announce that a memorandum of understanding has been signed between the Republic of Kazakhstan and Network School.
"Our new campus will be a haven for the global technology-optimist community," he said.
In an Instagram post, Kazakhstan's Ministry of Digital Development, Innovation and Aerospace Industry said the MoU was signed by Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Digital Development, Innovation and Aerospace Industry Zhaslan Madiyev and Balaji.
The collaboration will include the development of artificial intelligence education programmes, support for start-ups, joint research initiatives and the organisation of international conferences.
It also aims to attract leading experts, technology companies and foreign investment to Kazakhstan, strengthening the country's position as a regional hub for artificial intelligence and innovation.
The announcement came after the Iskandar Puteri City Council (MBIP) revoked the business licence of NSO Malaysia Sdn Bhd, which operated Network School, and ordered it to cease all activities from today after finding it had breached several licensing conditions.
The Johor government said the decision followed a special full council meeting, which found the company had failed to comply with conditions imposed under its business licence.
Among the breaches identified were operating one of its premises without a valid licence and failing to comply with conditions governing the use of the premises.
Network School
came under scrutiny after the Johor government raised concerns over allegations that Israeli nationals had joined the programme in Forest City using passports issued by other countries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Madani economy on right track as Malaysia moves up competitiveness rankings - Samenta
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's climb of eight places to 15th in the International Institute for Management Development (IMD) World Competitiveness Ranking demonstrates that the MADANI Economy framework is on the right track, particularly through the government's commitment to reducing bureaucratic red tape.
Small and Medium Enterprises Association of Malaysia (SAMENTA) president Datuk William Ng said the country's best performance in more than a decade highlights the economy's true potential when the public and private sectors work closely together.
He said that the improvements in administrative processes under the Reformasi Kerenah Birokrasi (Bureaucratic Red Tape Reform or RKB) initiative, together with the enactment of the Government Service Efficiency Commitment Act 2025, known as the Iltizam Act, have delivered very positive results.
"This is reflected in government efficiency rising 11 places to 14th globally, while business efficiency climbed 16 places to 16th in the world," he said in a statement here today.
He said the Time to Start a Business indicator surged 38 places to 15th globally, while the Business Legislation indicator rose 29 places to sixth in the world, driven by clearer and more structured regulatory compliance.
Ng said the country's resilience and agility in adapting to changes in global supply chains amid worldwide energy challenges and geo-economic uncertainty are the result of close collaboration between the government and industry.
To sustain the positive momentum, he proposed that efforts to reduce bureaucratic red tape be extended to local authorities (PBTs) and statutory bodies.
He said a SAMENTA survey found that small and medium enterprises (SMEs) continue to face unpredictable processing times for licences, permits and renewals at the PBT level, contributing to higher operating costs and business uncertainty.
He added that SAMENTA has urged the government to set a benchmark requiring at least 40 per cent local supply chain participation in major foreign direct investment (FDI) projects, particularly in high-technology sectors.
"Such a move is important to ensure that investments by multinational companies also support the sustainable development of local SME capabilities," he said.
On the role of the Malaysia Productivity Corporation (MPC), he proposed that the Productivity Nexus be strengthened from a policy bridge into a dedicated implementation unit working with business associations to deliver digitalisation and productivity enhancement programmes specifically for micro and small businesses.
He said the country's improved competitiveness ranking is a victory for all Malaysians, but public-private collaboration at the local level must continue to be strengthened to enable local businesses to compete on the global stage.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's AI ambitions require skilled ict workforce - Khairul Firdaus
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Malaysia's growing investments in artificial intelligence (AI), cloud computing and hyperscale data centres are driving demand for a highly skilled information and communications technology (ICT) workforce, says Deputy Minister of Human Resources Datuk Khairul Firdaus Akbar Khan.
In this regard Khairul Firdaus said the Ministry of Human Resources (Kesuma) continued to strengthen Malaysia's workforce by equipping Malaysians with the skills needed to thrive in an increasingly digital and AI-driven economy.
"Our focus is clear to ensure that Malaysians are not only ready for the jobs of today, but also equipped for the high-value careers of tomorrow.
"Our long-term competitiveness depends on our ability to develop Malaysians with the knowledge, technical expertise and professional competencies needed to seize these opportunities.
"Technology can be imported. Buildings can be constructed...Equipment can be purchased. But highly skilled talent must be nurtured. This is where Kesuma plays an important role," he said when officiating the two-day BICSI Southeast Asia Conference and Exhibition Malaysia 2026, here today.
Also present at the conference were BICSI chief executive officer John H. Daniels and director of BICSI Southeast Asia Affiliate Mary Elizabeth Cheng.
The conference brings together ICT infrastructure professionals, engineers, consultants, technology providers, policymakers and business leaders from across Southeast Asia to discuss AI-ready infrastructure, advanced fibre connectivity, structured cabling, edge computing, cybersecurity, smart buildings and sustainable digital infrastructure.
According to Khairul Firdaus, initiatives such as TalentCorp's Jelajah AI MyMahir (JAM) programme were equipping Malaysians with practical AI knowledge and industry-relevant digital skills to remain competitive in an increasingly technology-driven workplace.
"Behind every data centre, every intelligent building, every fibre network and every AI application is something more important, which is skilled people. Infrastructure enables innovation and talent sustains it.
"That is why this conference is not merely about technology. It is about developing the people who will design, build, secure, operate and continuously improve the digital economy of tomorrow," he said.
--BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia set to lead global ESG sukuk issuance in 2H26: Fitch
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is expected to lead global environmental, social and governance (ESG) sukuk issuance in the second half of 2026, supported by tax incentives, strong investor demand and a deep domestic market.
Fitch Ratings said Malaysia-based issuers accounted for 67 per cent of global ESG sukuk issuance in the first half of 2026. All issuances were denominated in ringgit and nearly tripled from a year earlier.
The energy, utilities and transportation sectors led the issuance, it said in a statement today.
Fitch expects full-year global ESG sukuk issuance to remain below 2025 levels, in line with a broader slowdown in the global sukuk market amid volatility stemming from the Middle East.
Issuance in the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) is also expected to moderate.
The firm said ESG sukuk growth could be affected by shifting sustainability priorities, increasingly complex and evolving Syariah and ESG requirements, changes in investor sentiment, and greenwashing risks.
Globally, ESG sukuk issuance fell 32 per cent year-on-year to US$4.9 billion in the first half of 2026 amid Iran war-related volatility and rising yields, marking the first decline in first-half issuance in four years.
Its share of dollar-denominated emerging market ESG debt, excluding China, also fell to 14 per cent from 41 per cent a year earlier, as dollar ESG sukuk issuance plunged 74 per cent while dollar ESG bond issuance rose 11.5 per cent.
Despite weaker issuance, outstanding ESG sukuk rose about 24 per cent to more than US$61 billion at end-June 2026. Malaysia accounted for 31 per cent of the total, followed by Saudi Arabia (27 per cent), the United Arab Emirates (20.2 per cent) and Indonesia (15.3 per cent).
Fitch said ESG sukuk outpaced the growth of ESG bonds outstanding in Malaysia, Saudi Arabia and Indonesia, while accounting for more than 12 per cent of global dollar-denominated sukuk outstanding.
About 95 per cent of Fitch-rated ESG sukuk were investment grade at end-June, with 49.6 per cent rated in the "A" category and 41.6 per cent in the "BBB" category.
Issuers with a "Stable Outlook" remained dominant at 74 per cent. However, those with a "Negative Outlook" rose to 19 per cent, mainly involving Indonesia, while securities on Rating Watch Negative increased to six per cent, largely involving United Arab Emirates corporates and a Qatari bank.
Fitch-rated dollar ESG sukuk accounted for around 11 per cent of all rated dollar sukuk, equivalent to US$25.2 billion. Fitch also rated more than 65 per cent of global dollar ESG sukuk.
The agency said ESG sukuk made up 72 per cent of Malaysia's hard-currency ESG debt, followed by Indonesia at 51 per cent and the GCC at 44 per cent.
By comparison, ESG sukuk remained marginal in Turkiye at about three per cent and had yet to emerge in Pakistan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 44,018 complaints against police recorded from 2020 to 2025
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 44,018 complaints and information were received by the police's Integrity and Standards Compliance Department (JIPS) nationwide between 2020 and 2025.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the highest number was recorded last year, with 10,294 complaints, followed by 7,675 in 2021, 6,696 in 2024, 6,525 in 2022, 6,440 in 2023 and 6,388 in 2020.
He said the largest number of complaints and information was received by Bukit Aman, with 27,566 cases, followed by the Kuala Lumpur police contingent with 2,111 and Selangor with 2,082.
Perlis recorded the lowest number, with 228 complaints and information received during the period.
"Integrity-related complaints were the highest recorded during the period, with 23,186 complaints, followed by governance-related complaints (5,229), corruption-related complaints (894) and general complaints (14,709)," he said in a written parliamentary reply.
Saifuddin said 8,044 complaints were classified as No Further Action (NFA) after they were found to have no basis or insufficient evidence for investigation.
"The remaining 35,974 complaints were brought for further investigation by the police," he said.
On the management of detainees, Saifuddin said the government remained committed to strengthening procedures in line with recommendations by the Enforcement Agency Integrity Commission (EAIC).
He said the police were open to recommendations and continued to implement improvements to ensure the welfare, safety and human rights of detainees were protected.
Among the measures taken was the expansion of closed-circuit television (CCTV) usage at all police lock-ups at station and district levels to enable 24-hour monitoring of detainees' movements and physical condition.
He said the police also required every detainee to undergo an initial health screening before being placed in lock-ups, in accordance with the Lock-up Rules, the Inspector-General of Police Standing Orders (PTKPN) and the Health Screening Guidelines for Detainees by Medical Practitioners at police lock-ups.
"Through this screening, any injuries, existing illnesses or health problems can be identified early, while detainees requiring further treatment will be referred immediately to the relevant healthcare facilities," he said.
He said the police had also strengthened training for officers and personnel assigned to lock-up duties, particularly on compliance with human rights, detainee-handling procedures, welfare management, and emergency response when detainees showed signs of illness.
He said the measures were part of the government's continuous efforts to improve lock-up and detention management in accordance with the law, existing procedures and best practices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah insists border control law must not erode state immigration powers
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The implementation of the Malaysia Border Control and Protection Agency Act 2024 (Act 860) in Sabah must not erode the state's autonomous immigration powers.
Assistant Minister to the Chief Minister Datuk Joniston Bangkuai said the Act falls under the federal government's jurisdiction.
However, he said the state government maintains that Sabah's immigration powers, as enshrined under Article V of the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63), are non-negotiable, as the formation of Malaysia was subject to the preservation of Sabah's and Sarawak's immigration authority.
"Accordingly, the Sabah government has formally conveyed its position to the Prime Minister, insisting that the Sabah Immigration Department remain the agency responsible for controlling all entry points into the state and that the authority to issue Not-To-Land (NTL) notices remain under the department's jurisdiction.
"The state government also emphasised that the constitutional safeguards protecting Sabah's position must always be preserved and that there should be no erosion of the state's special rights and autonomous immigration powers."
Joniston was replying to a question from Datuk Seri Dr Wilfred Madius Tangau (Upko-Tamparuli) on the reasons for the state government's delay in implementing the Act.
Meanwhile, assistant public works and utilities minister Datuk Seri Ruddy Awah said there were 17 cable theft cases in Kota Kinabalu and Karambunai between last year and June this year.
"Sabah Electricity views cable theft seriously as it not only causes financial losses but may also affect the reliability of electricity supply to consumers.
"In this regard, Sabah Electricity has strengthened preventive measures through regular patrols and monitoring in areas identified as high-risk locations, including conducting integrated operations with enforcement agencies," he said in reply to Datuk Aliakbar Gulasan (PAS-Karambunai), who asked about cable theft cases in his constituency.
Ruddy said 35 patrol and monitoring operations were carried out in the Karambunai constituency between January and June this year as a proactive measure to reduce cable theft incidents and strengthen enforcement efforts.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Psychological first aid vital to calm those going through crisis
Article:
LETTERS:
Crises can arrive without warning and change lives within seconds.
During an emergency, some people cry uncontrollably and  others become silent. Some well experience a mental block, like not remembering their own telephone number.
These are normal responses to extraordinary events.
After more than two decades as a crisis counsellor, counsellor educator and crisis intervention practitioner, I have learned that emergencies affect  thoughts, emotions, behaviours, relationships and even one's sense of meaning and hope.
Yet, Yet very few people are taught what happens psychologically during a crisis.
Research in disaster psychology finds that people who understand what to expect during emergencies cope more effectively than those who are unprepared.
Preparedness helps us recognise our reactions, manage them more effectively and continue functioning despite our fear.
Every airline safety briefing states that one should put on one's own oxygen mask before helping someone else. The same principle applies during emergencies.
A calm person thinks more clearly, notices important details, communicates more effectively and makes safer decisions.
Children look to adults to judge whether they are safe. Patients look to healthcare workers. Students look to teachers.  Communities look to leaders.
If those around them remain composed, hope becomes possible.
This is where Psychological First Aid (PFA) becomes invaluable.
The World Health Organisation describes PFA as humane, supportive and practical assistance provided to people experiencing serious distress following a crisis.
It is designed to reduce immediate emotional suffering, restore a sense of safety and hope, strengthen people's natural coping abilities and connect them with practical support and professional services when necessary.
PFA follows three simple principles: Look. Listen. Link.
The first step — look. Before we speak, we observe. Is the environment safe? Is there immediate danger? Does the person require urgent medical attention?
We also observe emotional and behavioural reactions.
Is the person overwhelmed by panic? Are they confused or disoriented? Are they unable to communicate? Listen to what they say.
Link means connecting people with the right services, whether medical, emergency, social welfare, family, community or mental health support.
Malaysia has made tremendous progress in strengthening physical emergency preparedness.
Schools conduct fire drills. Communities participate in disaster preparedness programmes. CPR training has become increasingly accessible.
Perhaps the next step is to place equal importance on psychological preparedness.
PFA is a way of responding to another human being with compassion, dignity and respect when they are at their most vulnerable.
ASSOCIATE PROFESSOR DR HANIZA RAIS
Executive Committee member
PERKAMA International
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt aligns investment direction of GLICs, public funds through GEAR-uP, MoF Inc
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is aligning the investment direction of government-linked investment companies (GLICs) and public funds through a clear national development policy, the Government-linked Enterprises Activation and Reform Programme (GEAR-uP) mechanism, and the strategic role of the Minister of Finance Incorporated.
Finance deputy minister Liew Chin Tong said this alignment is carried out without undermining each institution's investment mandate, governance, and fiduciary responsibilities.
"The full progress of this programme will be reported comprehensively and transparently in the GEAR-uP progress report, which will be published soon.
"The government's objective is to ensure that the nation's institutional capital delivers lasting capabilities: sustainable industries, competitive companies, and more secure livelihoods for the people," he told the Senate during today's question-and-answer session.
Liew was responding to Senator Datuk Rosni Sohar's question regarding the government's policy and mechanisms for aligning the investment direction of GLICs and public funds with national development priorities.
In response to Rosni's supplementary question about mechanisms to ensure GLIC investments also benefit micro, small and medium enterprises, Liew said the government hopes the GEAR-uP programme, together with the New Industrial Masterplan 2030 (NIMP 2030) and the National Semiconductor Strategy, will develop a more sustainable and dynamic industry in the long term.
He said the government intends to leverage investments from GLICs and government-linked companies, while also attracting participation from funds and local and foreign investors (crowding in), to enable Malaysia to develop its own technologies.
"Although they may not be the most advanced, we want to become an indispensable middle player in the global supply chain by holding choke points and developing our own industries and technologies. In doing so, we can strengthen the entire ecosystem, including supporting local small and medium enterprises," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt aligns investment direction of GLICs, public funds through GEAR uP, MOF Inc
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is aligning the investment direction of government-linked investment companies (GLICs) and public funds through a clear national development policy, the Government-linked Enterprises Activation and Reform Programme (GEAR-uP) mechanism, and the strategic role of the Minister of Finance Incorporated (MOF Inc).
Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong said this alignment is carried out without undermining each institution's investment mandate, governance, and fiduciary responsibilities.
"The full progress of this programme will be reported comprehensively and transparently in the GEAR-uP progress report, which will be published soon.
"The government's objective is to ensure that the nation's institutional capital delivers lasting capabilities: sustainable industries, competitive companies, and more secure livelihoods for the people," he told the Senate during today's question-and-answer session.
Liew was responding to Senator Datuk Rosni Sohar's question regarding the government's policy and mechanisms for aligning the investment direction of GLICs and public funds with national development priorities.
In response to Rosni's supplementary question about mechanisms to ensure GLIC investments also benefit micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs), Liew said the government hopes the GEAR-uP programme, together with the New Industrial Masterplan 2030 (NIMP 2030) and the National Semiconductor Strategy (NSS), will develop a more sustainable and dynamic industry in the long term.
He said the government intends to leverage investments from GLICs and government-linked companies (GLCs), while also attracting participation from funds and local and foreign investors (crowding in), to enable Malaysia to develop its own technologies.
"Although they may not be the most advanced, we want to become an indispensable middle player in the global supply chain by holding choke points and developing our own industries and technologies. In doing so, we can strengthen the entire ecosystem, including supporting local SMEs," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fancy winning RM3,500 a month for life?
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Nestlé (Malaysia) Bhd has brought back its "Gaji Seumur Hidup" campaign for a third edition, strengthening consumer engagement while continuing its support for charitable causes.
The campaign, which runs from Aug 1 to Sept 30, offers prizes worth more than RM4 million.
These include a grand prize of RM3,500 a month for life, two first prizes of RM3,500 a month for two years and 18 consolation prizes of RM3,500 a month for one year.
A further 10,000 winners will receive RM100 Touch 'n Go eWallet reload pins through weekly draws.
Nestle Malaysia chief executive officer Juan Aranols said the campaign reflects the company's commitment to creating shared value by supporting consumers and communities.
"Gaji Seumur Hidup holds a special place in our hearts because it reflects the hopes, aspirations and everyday realities of Malaysians.
"What makes it meaningful is its ripple effect beyond the winners through support for charitable organisations," he said at the campaign launch in Kuala Lumpur.
Nestlé Malaysia will also contribute RM900,000 to 21 charitable organisations nationwide under this year's campaign.
First introduced in 2020 during the Covid-19 pandemic, Gaji Seumur Hidup returned in 2024 following strong public response.
Across the previous two editions, Nestlé Malaysia awarded more than RM8 million in cash prizes and channelled RM1.5 million to 35 charitable organisations.
The initiative aims to celebrate Malaysian aspirations while creating opportunities to improve the quality of life of individuals and communities.
Consumers can participate by buying a minimum of RM20 worth of participating Nestlé products in a single receipt and submitting their entries through the campaign's online channels.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK's Aberdeen Group emerges as substantial shareholder in MTT Shipping
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: United Kingdom-based Aberdeen Group plc has emerged as a substantial shareholder in MTT Shipping and Logistics Bhd after its Malaysian investment management unit bought a 5.08 per cent stake in the company.
Bursa Malaysia filings showed that abrdn Malaysia Sdn Bhd purchased 126.86 million shares in MTT Shipping on July 20, representing a 5.075 per cent direct interest.
Separate filings showed that Aberdeen Group and abrdn Holdings Ltd each hold a deemed interest in the same block of shares through abrdn Malaysia.
According to Bursa Malaysia shareholding data, individual investors collectively own 53.10 per cent of MTT Shipping, while corporate shareholders account for 21.44 per cent.
Executive chairman Datuk Seri Ong Kean Lee remains the company's largest shareholder with a 22.39 per cent stake, equivalent to 559.67 million shares.
He is followed by Malaysia Trade and Transport Company Sdn Bhd, which owns 14.66 per cent, or 366.40 million shares.
MTT Shipping shares were unchanged at 96.5 sen at the midday break, valuing the company at RM2.41 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bangkok pub fire death toll rises to 35, medical centre says
Article:
BANGKOK: The death toll from a July 13 fire at a pub in the Thai capital Bangkok rose to 35 on Wednesday, the city's emergency services said.
Twenty-one people were still in hospital, with 12 still in critical condition, the Erawan Emergency Medical Centre said.
Another 48 people injured in the blaze have been discharged, the centre added.
An explosive fire at the Rong Beer Na Lat Phrao pub broke out shortly before midnight on July 13 and filled the popular venue with thick smoke within minutes, Reuters has reported.
Thai police are investigating possible negligence after emergency exits were found to have been obstructed, preventing people from escaping.
Police authorities did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment on the progress of the investigation.
On Monday, Thai police said no charges had been filed because the venue's owner was still in hospital.
The government vowed tighter regulations, including random inspections.
In the last three days, over 800 venues have been inspected, with 56 ordered to suspend operations due to safety issues, Bangkok governor Chadchart Sittipunt said in a statement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MediAsas to complement, not replace, public healthcare system - Dzulkefly
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The upcoming MediAsas medical and health insurance/takaful plan will complement, rather than replace, the public healthcare system, which will continue to provide Universal Health Coverage (UHC) for Malaysians.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the plan was being developed as part of the Government's RESET Strategy to address medical claims inflation and improve the sustainability of healthcare financing.
"Importantly, the MediAsas plan currently being developed does not replace but complements the public healthcare system, which will continue to provide Universal Health Coverage (UHC) for all Malaysians," he said in a written parliamentary reply.
He said public healthcare services remained the cornerstone in ensuring access to quality healthcare for all Malaysians regardless of socioeconomic status, including middle-income groups, and would continue to be improved.
The RESET Strategy is being spearheaded by the Health Ministry, Finance Ministry and Bank Negara Malaysia through the Ministerial Joint Committee on Private Healthcare Costs, established last year.
One of the key strategic pillars under RESET is the development of a basic medical and health insurance/takaful (MHIT) plan, now known as MediAsas, which is targeted for launch in January 2027 with affordable premiums.
Dzulkefly said the MediAsas White Paper was published in January 2026, while updates on RESET's implementation had been communicated periodically through the media and official communication channels.
He said other initiatives under RESET included improving electronic medical record interoperability to reduce duplication of medical tests and scans, restructuring private hospital bills, and publishing price ranges for commonly used private healthcare services in Malaysia in January 2026 to help Malaysians make informed decisions.
The ministry also welcomed bipartisan support from the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) and Parliamentary Special Select Committee (PSSC) for the Government's efforts under the RESET Strategy.
He said MPs had acknowledged and supported a "whole-of-nation approach" to flatten medical claims inflation, which was a global challenge.
The government will pilot
MediAsas
by the end of this month ahead of its nationwide rollout in January next year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fatal Klang crash: Defence lawyer mulls representation to A-GC
Article:
KLANG: The lawyer representing a technician accused of causing a fatal crash while driving under the influence is considering submitting a representation to the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC).
Defence lawyer Aravind Raj said he would first review all prosecution documents relating to his client R. Saktygaanapathy's charge of self-administering drugs before deciding on the proposed representation.
A representation is typically submitted to seek a withdrawal or reduction of the charge against an accused.
During mention proceedings today, prosecuting officer Inspector Mohamad Rabiul Awal Ishak informed magistrate Nor Azilah Mat that the prosecution had handed over the relevant documents, including the laboratory report, to the defence.
The court fixed Aug 28 for mention to update the status of the representation.
Saktygaanapathy, 28, claimed trial to a revised charge of self-administering tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) at the Narcotics Criminal Investigation Department of the Klang Selatan district police headquarters at 3.30pm on March 29.
The charge, framed under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, carries a maximum fine of RM5,000, a jail term of up to two years and police supervision for up to three years upon conviction.
Dressed in a black T-shirt, Saktygaanapathy was brought from Sungai Buloh Prison for the proceedings.
Meanwhile, he also appeared before magistrate A. Karthiyayini in connection with a separate charge of murdering delivery rider Amirul Hafiz Omar, 32.
Deputy public prosecutor Salman Alfarisi Nawawi informed the court that the prosecution was still awaiting the pathology report.
Karthiyayini fixed Oct 7 for further mention of the case.
Saktygaanapathy is accused of causing Amirul's death along Jalan Raya Barat here at 11.47am on March 29.
No plea was recorded as the murder charge falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
The charge, framed under Section 302 of the Penal Code, carries the death penalty or imprisonment of between 30 and 40 years upon conviction.
If the death penalty is not imposed, the offender must be whipped at least 12 times.
Lawyer Nur Iffah Hizwani Omar held a watching brief on behalf of Amirul's widow, Nor Nadia Abdul Majid, 32, who attended the proceedings with several family members.
According to police investigations, Saktygaanapathy allegedly crashed his car head-on into Amirul, who was riding a motorcycle while on his way to buy a mathematics book for his children.
Amirul was pronounced dead at the scene after being struck by the car driven by the accused, who was allegedly travelling at high speed towards Klang town from Jalan Raya Barat.
He was believed to have overtaken several vehicles before veering into the opposite lane and colliding head-on with Amirul's motorcycle.
The impact flung Amirul onto a Perodua Myvi. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
He was buried at the Kampung Repoh Islamic Cemetery in Larut, Perak.
He is survived by his wife and their three children, Muhammad Aqif Amsyar, nine; Muhammad Afif Izz Rayqal, seven; and Nur Humaira, two.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sarawak Day: Anwar lauds progress, reaffirms federal ties
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said close cooperation between the federal government and the Sarawak administration has strengthened the state's position as a driver of growth, innovation and inclusive development.
In his Sarawak Day message today, Anwar said every effort was being driven towards elevating the people's livelihoods, strengthening the economy and ensuring sustainable prosperity.
He said the transformation and progress taking place in Sarawak were the result of collective efforts, with the spirit of Sarawak Maju Segulai Sejalai continuing to drive unity, development and a more prosperous future for the state and Malaysia.
"Through the spirit of Sarawak Maju Segulai Sejalai, we will continue to move forward together in strengthening unity, accelerating development and building a more prosperous future for Sarawak and Malaysia," he said.
Anwar also expressed hope that Sarawak, known as the Land of the Hornbills, would continue to achieve success, drive prosperity and remain a source of national pride.
"May Bumi Kenyalang continue to record achievements, spur prosperity and become the pride of the nation, for a brighter future for all Sarawakians and Malaysians," he said.
Sarawak Day is celebrated every July 22 to mark the state's attainment of self-government in 1963, ahead of the formation of Malaysia later that year.
This year marks the 63rd anniversary of the milestone.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTViral: Kuantan beach turns magical as rare bioluminescent waves draw crowds [WATCH]
Article:
KUANTAN: A stunning bioluminescent phenomenon has drawn large crowds to Pantai Tembeling here as visitors flock to catch a glimpse of its unique glowing waves.
Social media has been abuzz with images of the natural spectacle, which causes stretches of the shoreline to appear luminous due to bioluminescent plankton in the water.
Over the past week, large crowds have gathered at the beach to experience and capture the sparkling waves, sharing videos and images of water glistening like stars in the night sky.
Tourism Pahang took to Facebook to promote the 'blue tears phenomenon', describing it as a spectacle that transforms the nighttime scenery into something truly magical with its natural blue glow produced by microscopic marine organisms.
"Have you ever witnessed a glowing ocean at night? The mesmerising phenomenon of bioluminescence has now been reported near Tembeling Beach, Kuantan.
"If you have the opportunity to witness this phenomenon, appreciate this natural wonder by helping to keep the beach clean and protect the surrounding ecosystem," it wrote on Facebook.
Bioluminescence is a natural process where plankton produce and emit light when disturbed by movement, causing the water to appear to glow - often with an electric blue hue - especially at night.
Pantai Tembeling has emerged as a new hotspot for sightings, joining other popular locations such as Kuala Selangor, Pulau Sembilan and Bagan Datuk in Perak, as well as Miri in Sarawak.
The unique phenomenon here has prompted netizens to share images on social media, with some saying the surge in visitors has been a blessing for traders operating near the beach, allowing them to extend their business hours until the early hours of the morning.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tremors felt along west coast after Sumatra quake [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tremors were felt along the west coast of Peninsular Malaysia following a moderate earthquake in northern Sumatra today.
The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) said the 5.7-magnitude earthquake occurred at 12.18pm, with the epicentre located at coordinates 4.2° North and 97.6° East.
"The earthquake was recorded 55km southwest of Langsa, Indonesia, at a depth of 10km," it said in a Facebook post.
Those who experienced shaking from the earthquake are encouraged to provide feedback by completing the survey form at https://forms.gle/3BiFefhGT4yysDiC9
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Noor'azah determined to champion women's voices in Ampangan
Article:
SEREMBAN: Bersatu's Ampangan seat candidate Noor'azah Harun is determined to champion and amplify the voices of women in the constituency if elected.
The 48-year-old said the positive support and reception she had received throughout the campaign, particularly from women, had strengthened her resolve to serve the community.
"Women have responded positively to the campaign and the issues I am advocating, especially those affecting single mothers.
"I know many women, particularly single mothers, are reluctant to come forward. Through this approach, I hope to help improve their quality of life," she said after a walkabout at Flat Uda in Bandar Baru Ampangan, here.
Noor'azah is in a three-cornered contest against former Ampangan assemblyman Datuk Dr Rafie Abd Malek of Perikatan Nasional and Datuk Muhammad Nazri Kassim of Pakatan Harapan.
She said several issues had been raised by residents, including complaints from traders at Benteng Ampangan.
"They told me many shop lots are vacant because new applications take a long time to be processed due to multiple approval stages.
"When many shops remain empty, visitor numbers also decline, causing the area to lose its vibrancy even though it is one of Ampangan's commercial hubs.
"There are also claims that many traders there are from outside Ampangan and we will try to help resolve the matter," she said.
Although contesting for the first time, Noor'azah expressed confidence in her ability to compete against more established opponents, citing her years of grassroots political experience.
"I respect the other candidates who have more experience, but being a newcomer is not a weakness.
"I am proud to contest under the Bersatu logo in this election.
"I will not give up and will fight until the very end. Being chosen as a candidate is the proudest moment of my 10 years in politics," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cattle herder remanded for four days over alleged police assault
Article:
BALIK PULAU: A 43-year-old cattle herder has been remanded for four days to assist investigations into allegedly obstructing and assaulting a policeman.
Magistrate Chia Huey Ting issued the remand order until July 25 to facilitate investigations under Section 186 of the Penal Code for obstructing a public servant in the discharge of their public functions.
The incident allegedly occurred yesterday at the Balik Pulau public market when a police Mobile Patrol Vehicle team spotted the suspect's motorcycle parked beside the food court while he was behaving suspiciously.
Police said the suspect failed to cooperate during an inspection, leading to an argument before he assaulted a policeman by striking him in the chest.
The man was arrested and taken to the Southwest police headquarters for further investigation.
The suspect was previously detained in 2024 for a similar offence involving the alleged assault of a policeman during an inspection.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WD Malaysia bets on sustainable storage as AI data demand surges
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Nasdaq-listed Western Digital Corp (WDC) is stepping up efforts to make artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure more sustainable as global demand for data storage accelerates, focusing on higher storage efficiency, lower energy consumption and reduced physical infrastructure requirements.
"As AI infrastructure expands around the world, innovation in storage efficiency plays an increasingly important role in reducing the environmental impact associated with managing exponentially growing volumes of data," the company said in a statement.
WDC is focusing on increasing storage density and improving power efficiency to enable customers to scale AI infrastructure more sustainably while lowering operational costs.
Dr Chandramogan Anamirtham, senior vice-president of global operations at WDC, said responsible manufacturing has become increasingly important as demand for AI-driven data storage grows.
"As global demand for AI-driven data storage accelerates, our commitment to responsible manufacturing has never been more critical," he said.
Beyond environmental targets, WDC said it continues to invest in operational excellence and workforce development across Malaysia as it supports the growing global demand for advanced data storage technologies.
WDC's Malaysia operations (WD Malaysia) form part of its broader global sustainability roadmap, which includes achieving 100 per cent carbon-free energy by financial year 2030 (FY30) and net zero Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by FY32.
The company's Sarawak facility has already advanced its renewable energy transition through a Renewable Energy Certificate (REC) agreement with Sarawak Energy Bhd, enabling 100 per cent Scope 2 market-based emissions reduction since FY24.
WD Malaysia has been recognised for their sustainability efforts, receiving three awards across corporate, leadership and practitioner categories at the Sustainability & CSR Malaysia Awards 2026 and Asia Sustainability Personality Awards 2026.
It received the Company of the Year Award (Manufacturing & Technology) under the Green Innovation & Sustainability Transformation category at the Sustainability & CSR Malaysia Awards 2026, recognising its efforts to integrate sustainability across its manufacturing operations in Sarawak, Penang and Johor.
The recognition comes as the rapid expansion of AI workloads drives unprecedented growth in global data generation, increasing pressure on technology companies to develop more energy-efficient storage solutions.
The company's sustainability strategy is centred on helping customers store larger volumes of data while reducing the environmental footprint associated with AI infrastructure, including energy consumption, cooling requirements and physical data centre space.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illegal houses on state land rented to Rohingya for higher rents, says Kelantan exco
Article:
KOTA BARU: The Kelantan government believes many villagers who have illegally built houses on state land are renting them out to foreigners, including Rohingya, attracted by higher rental returns.
State Local Government, Housing, Health and Environment Committee chairman Hilmi Abdullah said some locals chose to rent their houses to undocumented migrants because they were willing to pay higher rents and were punctual with their monthly payments.
He said the trend had become a concern as some residents were taking advantage of the strong rental demand from undocumented migrants.
"They are willing to offer higher rental rates and are very punctual in paying rent every month.
"That is one of the reasons some locals choose to rent out these houses to them.
"Some of the structures that were demolished were old houses built by villagers on government land.
"We took action because they were illegal structures on state land and were being rented out to Rohingya," he said when contacted.
Hilmi said enforcement action was only taken against illegal structures erected on government land.
"If the house is built on privately owned land, the owner has the right to rent it out and we cannot take action under the state land laws.
"Our action is limited to illegal structures built on government land.
"If a house is occupied by the owner or another Malaysian family, we do not demolish it simply because it is on state land.
"The issue arises when illegal structures are used to accommodate undocumented migrants," he said.
He said the Kelantan government had not detected any organised illegal foreign settlements in the state so far, adding that enforcement and monitoring would continue to prevent such settlements from emerging.
Hilmi was commenting on yesterday's demolition of several illegal structures on government land in Tanah Merah that had been occupied by Rohingya families.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't exploit divisive issues, Aminuddin warns political parties in Negri polls
Article:
PORT DICKSON: Political parties contesting the Negri Sembilan state election have been urged not to exploit issues that could undermine racial unity in the state.
Caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun said the harmony enjoyed by the multi-ethnic communities in the state must be preserved and not exploited for political gain.
"Negri Sembilan has long enjoyed good understanding among its multi-racial communities. We do not want sensitive issues to arise that could lead to division.
"People of different backgrounds live together peacefully. I do not see any reason for racial issues to be exploited to create division or to advance the interests of any political party," he told reporters during a campaign event in Linggi.
Aminuddin, who is also Pakatan Harapan's candidate for the Linggi seat, said he was confident voters would be able to assess the coalition's eight-year track record in governing Negri Sembilan.
He said PH's manifesto for the state election demonstrated the coalition's commitment to delivering on its promises for the well-being of the people.
"For the past eight years, Negri Sembilan has remained aligned with the federal government.
"During that period, we went through various political changes. PH initially formed the federal government, but the Sheraton Move later placed us in the opposition.
"However, being in the opposition did not stop us from carrying out our responsibilities. Alhamdulillah, since PH returned to power at the federal level in 2023, this alignment has enabled Negri Sembilan to continue progressing," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Keropok seller charged with using drone to smuggle tobacco into prison
Article:
MELAKA: A keropok seller was charged in the magistrate's court here with criminal trespass and smuggling tobacco into Sungai Udang Prison using an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), or drone, last week.
Mohd Nizam Ab Manaf, 46, pleaded not guilty when the charges were read to him.
On the first charge, Nizam is accused of committing criminal trespass with the intention to smuggle contraband tobacco into the Sungai Udang Prison compound using a drone under his control.
The offence was allegedly committed at No. 1, Jalan KS 6, Taman Kris Satria, Sungai Udang, about 1.20am on July 15.
The charge, framed under Section 447 of the Penal Code, carries a maximum sentence of six months' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM3,000, or both, upon conviction.
On the second charge, Nizam is accused of smuggling prohibited tobacco into Sungai Udang Prison using a drone under his control at the same time, date, and location.
The charge is brought under Section 58 of the Prisons Act 1995, which carries a maximum fine of RM10,000, imprisonment for up to five years, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Siti Nur Aliya Safri proposed bail at RM10,000 for each charge, alongside an additional condition requiring the accused to report to the nearest police station monthly until the case concludes.
National Legal Aid Foundation lawyer Muhammad Izzuddin Ab Malek, representing the accused, pleaded for lower bail, stating that Nizam, a native of Bachok, Kelantan, supports his wife and child by selling keropok with a monthly income of RM1,700.
The court granted bail at RM7,000 with one surety, subject to the prosecution's additional conditions, and fixed Aug 19 for case mention for legal representation and document submission.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NY mayor says city doesn't have authority to arrest Netanyahu on ICC warrant
Article:
NEW YORK: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani said Tuesday his office does not have the authority to carry out the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, and called on US federal officials to step in.
"My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here," Mamdani said in a brief video message posted to social media.
"It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant."
The Hague-based International Criminal Court said in 2024 that it had reasonable grounds to believe Netanyahu was responsible for alleged war crimes and crimes against humanity related to Israel's offensive in Gaza following the Oct 7, 2023 cross-border attack by Hamas.
US President Donald Trump on Monday vowed the Israeli Prime Minister would not face arrest if he attended September's United Nations General Assembly summit in New York.
In the video, Mamdani said "anyone – with their eyes, with their heart, with their conscience – should recognise the devastation he has wrought and understand that he belongs before a court of law, as I've said.
"I agree with the ICC that Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes, as I do for anyone else charged by the ICC," he added.
Netanyahu's office has dismissed Mamdani's stance and said the ICC is a kangaroo court.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTViral: Rider slammed for performing wheelie with woman, child on motorcycle
Article:
ALOR STAR: A motorcyclist has drawn widespread criticism after he was filmed performing a wheelie while carrying a woman and a child on his motorcycle at Alor Star City Square.
The 17-second clip, posted on Threads, showed the rider lifting the motorcycle's front wheel as he rode past the iconic Masjid Zahir and Balai Besar.
The stunt angered social media users, particularly as the child appeared not to be wearing a helmet.
Threads user @cerita.harini__, who uploaded the clip, sarcastically remarked that the rider had remembered to conceal the motorcycle's registration plate but appeared to have given little thought to the child's safety.
The comments section was quickly flooded with criticism, with many users calling on the police and the Road Transport Department (RTD) to take action.
"This is unbelievably foolish. The child was not even wearing a helmet. I am ashamed to see someone from Alor Star behaving like this," user @naimbadek wrote.
Another user, @rasydan_77, questioned what there was to be proud of, while @amirahusna90 said the video should be forwarded to the police, the RTD and the Social Welfare Department.
"Parents should know better. A child is a responsibility entrusted to them, not an object they can treat however they please," user @real4ira added.
The New Straits Times has reached out to the police and the RTD for comment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Defence weighs AGC appeal for driver in fatal Klang crash
Article:
KLANG, July 22 — The defence is exploring a formal representation to the Attorney-General’s Chambers (AGC) for a technician accused of driving under the influence and causing a fatal crash that killed a delivery rider.
Defence lawyer Aravind Raj stated that he is currently reviewing all prosecution documents, including laboratory reports, before deciding whether to submit the representation to seek a withdrawal or reduction of the charges,
NST
reported.
The move comes as the 28-year-old accused, R. Saktygaanapathy, pleaded not guilty to a charge of self-administering tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) at the Klang Selatan district police headquarters on March 29.
Escorted from Sungai Buloh Prison for the proceedings, the accused faces a maximum of two years in jail and a RM5,000 fine if convicted under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
The court has fixed August 28 for a mention to update the status of the representation.
While the drug charge is being contested, Saktygaanapathy faces a far more severe legal battle. He appeared before magistrate A. Karthiyayini in connection with the murder of 32-year-old delivery rider Amirul Hafiz Omar. Because the murder charge falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court, no plea was recorded.
The incident unfolded on March 29 along Jalan Raya Barat, where Saktygaanapathy allegedly drove at high speed, overtook several vehicles, and veered into the opposite lane. He struck Amirul’s motorcycle head-on with such force that the victim was hurled against a Perodua Myvi and pronounced dead at the scene.
The human cost of the collision was highlighted in court by the presence of Amirul’s widow, Nor Nadia Abdul Majid, and their three children, aged nine, seven, and two. Amirul had been on his way to buy a mathematics book for his children at the time of the crash.
If convicted under Section 302 of the Penal Code, Saktygaanapathy faces the death penalty or imprisonment between 30 and 40 years. If the death penalty is not imposed, the law mandates at least 12 strokes of the cane. The court has fixed October 7 for further mention of the murder case as the prosecution awaits the final pathology report.

Title: Amirudin: Pakatan banks on micro-campaign strategy to win over Negeri Sembilan voters
Article:
SEREMBAN, July 22 — Pakatan Harapan (PH) is focusing on a locality-based micro-campaign strategy to deliver the coalition’s message and election pledges directly to grassroots voters in the 16th Negeri Sembilan state election.
PH election director Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the small-group engagement approach is more effective as it focuses on addressing local issues and meeting the specific needs of each community, without touching on personal matters.
However, he said large-scale campaign events or mega ceramah still play an important role as the campaign finale, serving as a platform to showcase the strength and unity of the coalition machinery while attracting fence-sitters.
“In Negeri Sembilan, PH is leading the state government... the stability demonstrated during its first term will be a key selling point.
“PH has a strong track record, including increasing the state’s reserves and stimulating economic activity with growth of between 25 and 28 per cent. This will reinforce the confidence of opinion leaders and decision-makers to support the coalition with a strong majority,” he told Bernama here today.
Amirudin, who is also Selangor Menteri Besar, said the effectiveness of the micro-campaign strategy is further strengthened by PH’s well-established grassroots machinery in Negeri Sembilan, which has been firmly in place since the coalition was given the mandate to govern the state in 2018.
Commenting on improvements to the strategy compared with the recently concluded Johor state election, he said PH is placing greater emphasis this time on ensuring message consistency and strengthening coordination among the component parties’ campaign machinery to ensure a more organised campaign.
Beyond its campaign strategy, Amirudin is optimistic that PH’s administrative track record under Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun from 2018 to the present will be a key benchmark for voters in choosing political stability in the state.
On the cooperation between Barisan Nasional (BN) and Perikatan Nasional (PN) in the state election, Amirudin said the strategy reflects the new reality of the country’s political landscape, where parties have continued to seek the best coalition formula since the 2018 General Election.
He said past experience has shown that every political alliance faces its own challenges, particularly in maintaining consensus, allocating seats and aligning its direction when contesting elections.
Amirudin said the strength of a political coalition should not be judged solely on the outcome of a single election, but rather on its ability to sustain consensus and manage challenges over the long term.
In the 2023 Negeri Sembilan state election, PH won 17 seats, while BN secured 14 and PN five.
The Election Commission has set Aug 1 as polling day for the 16th Negeri Sembilan state election, with early voting scheduled for July 28.
For the latest updates on the 16th Negeri Sembilan state election, visit https://prn.bernama.com/n9. — Bernama

Title: Court of Appeal rules children born out of wedlock cannot inherit Malaysian father’s citizenship
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 22 — The Court of Appeal today unanimously decided that two persons who were born in Malaysia are not entitled to be Malaysian citizens under the Federal Constitution, as their biological Malaysian fathers were not married to their non-Malaysian mothers when they were born.
Court of Appeal judge Datuk Azhahari Kamal Ramli, who chaired a three-judge panel, said the High Court was correct in deciding to reject their bid to be recognised and declared as Malaysians.
Azhahari ultimately concluded that the Kuala Lumpur-born woman (identified only as J for privacy purposes) has to follow her Indonesian biological mother’s citizenship, and that the man born in Teluk Intan, Perak (identified only as D) has to follow his Filipino mother’s citizenship.
Both J’s and D’s biological non-Malaysian mothers have disappeared from their lives since they were young, and had studied and lived in Malaysia their whole lives while being raised by their Malaysian fathers.
Here’s a quick recap of the facts in their cases based on court documents, and what the Court of Appeal decided today:
The Court of Appeal said Malaysia-born children have to follow their mother's citizenship status, if the child is illegitimate or born when the parents were not married. — File picture by Devan Manuel
The case of J: Birth certificate once recorded her as Malaysian, had a Malaysian passport
J, who will be turning 30 this year, has a DNA test to show her biological ties to her Malaysian father.
Her Malaysian father has been caring for her since young, as her Indonesian mother had ran off and been uncontactable since she was three.
J’s initial birth certificate in 1996 was issued without stating her citizenship status, as this was not part of birth certificates’ format then.
J’s second birth certificate issued in 2004 in an updated format states that she is a Malaysian, and she was issued a Malaysian passport in 2006.
But when her father applied for her Malaysian identity card when she turned 12, J’s birth certificate was amended to show her status as “non-citizen” as her parents were unmarried at the time of her birth.
The Home Ministry rejected her citizenship applications four times in 2010, 2012, 2015 and 2021 without giving any reasons, while J said the National Registration Department (NRD) in 2023 did not allow her to apply for citizenship via naturalisation under the Federal Constitution’s Article 19.
The four citizenship applications that the Home Ministry had rejected were made under the Federal Constitution’s Article 15A (which J could only use before she hit the age limit of 21), while the Article 19 route was the only citizenship route available to her once she turned 21.
The NRD had in court documents denied not allowing J to even try to apply for citizenship under Article 19, as it said there were no documents that could prove that she applied or that the NRD had made any decision.
J says she has no ties to Indonesia and is now stateless, and a letter from the Indonesian Embassy here certified that she is not registered as an Indonesian citizen.
D had unsuccessfully tried to apply for Malaysian citizenship several times before coming to court. — Picture by Sayuti Zainudin
What about D’s case?
D, who will turn 28 this year, said his biological Filipino mother left him when he was just a few months old, and said he does not know where she is and had never been to the Philippines.
D said he only had his Malaysian biological father his whole life, but is now alone as his father had died in 2022.
D also has a DNA test confirming that he is the biological son of his Malaysian father.
D also has a letter from the Philippine Embassy in Kuala Lumpur which certified that his birth had never been reported there and that he had never applied or been issued a Philippine passport.
D said his future is uncertain and he cannot live a normal life as a stateless person in Malaysia, and said ignoring his father’s Malaysian citizenship status because his parents were not married is gender discrimination.
An SPM graduate, D said he faces difficulties as a stateless person, such as to continue his studies, to get a driving licence, to open bank accounts or apply for bank loans, to do online transactions, or to find work.
But applying all the same reasons in J’s case, Azhahari also read out the Court of Appeal’s unanimous decision to reject D’s citizenship bid under Section 1(a) and Section 1(e), and also said D is not stateless as he could trace his citizenship to his Filipino mother.
The Court of Appeal’s decision today was largely based on a Federal Court’s 2021 decision in CTEB’s citizenship case, as well as past Court of Appeal decisions in other citizenship cases.
Lawyer Larissa Ann Louis represented both J and D, while federal counsel Ng Wee Li appeared today for the National Registration Department’s director-general, the Home Ministry’s secretary-general and the Malaysian government.
The other two judges on the panel today are Datuk Faizah Jamaludin and Datuk Hayatul Akmal Abdul Aziz.
Last month, the same Court of Appeal panel had also rejected the citizenship appeals of three Malaysia-born children in similar situations of being born when their Malaysian fathers were not married to their non-Malaysian mothers.
They can still appeal to the Federal Court against the Court of Appeal’s decision, and one of them — Haritharan Mugunthan — had already confirmed via his lawyer that he would be appealing.
Recommended reading:
Stateless his whole life, 38-year-old Perak man wants to be Malaysian; says late mother born pre-Merdeka should have been citizen too
In court, Ipoh-born man questions why footballers granted citizenship, while he still waits despite having Malaysian father
